title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Project 3
text:
Due: Sunday, April 12, by the time listed in Canvas.
In this project you will practice tools from the descriptive statistics section of your toolbox. The purpose of descriptive statistics is summarizing and visualizing data as they appear in your dataset, without trying to generalize to a larger population. You can either use a dataset from TidyTuesday or find another one of your choice. The only requirement is that your dataset has at least 4 interesting variables and at least 10 rows. Once you have decided on a dataset, choose four variables, classify each as numerical or categorical, and describe them with univariate and/or bivariate tools we used in lecture and lab. From among these, you write up your most interesting findings in a one page report.
What to turn in on Canvas.
Submit three things:

Short answers (text box) Part A below (five brief questions).

One-page summary (file upload): A single PDF (or Google doc Word exported to PDF) that follows the Part B rubric.

R code (file upload): A notebook that runs from top to bottom and contains all code you used to load the data, explore the four variables, and make the figures and tables that appear (or that support what you report) on your one-pager.

If your notebook needs extra files (e.g. a downloaded CSV), zip the notebook and data together or document clearly in the notebook how to obtain the same file (e.g. TidyTuesday week and read_csv URL).




Pick one TidyTuesday dataset for a week you like. Include a link to the dataset’s README or raw data in your write-up and in Part A. You may use a dataset from another source if it contains at least 4 columns of interest and at least 10 rows. Clearly document where this dataset may be found.
Choose four variables from your dataset to focus on. For each variable, state whether it is numerical or categorical .
Summarize each variable appropriately:

Categorical: counts and proportions and bar charts or pie chart.

Numerical: at least one measure of center and one of spread (e.g. mean and SD, or median and IQR), plus plots such as histograms or boxplots as appropriate.

Explore relationships between variables using bivariate methods from class (e.g. scatterplot for two numerical variables; side-by-side boxplots or 100% stacked barplots for two categorical variables).
Pick the two most informative graphics for your one-page summary. Each must have a clear title and axis or legend labels (with units if applicable).
Write the one-page summary (Part B rubric). It must include your two chosen figures, at least four clearly labeled numeric summaries (e.g. two means and two SDs, or medians and IQRs, or counts/proportions expressed as numbers: enough that a reader sees four distinct quantitative facts), and a short paragraph of your four most interesting findings.





Answer briefly in Canvas:

Which dataset did you use? Briefly give the context of this dataset.

How many variables (columns) are in the full dataset?

Which four variables did you choose? What are their types?

How many observations (rows) are in the full dataset?

Was there missing data in any of your four chosen variables? If yes, say which variable(s) and how you handled or reported it.












Points
Criterion




4
Short paragraph: what the dataset is, where it came from, a research or exploratory question, how many variables and how many observations (in the full dataset).


8
Two graphs, chosen as the most informative/interesting out of all the ones you created; each has an informative title and axis/legend labels (units where needed).


8
At least four numerical summaries with clear labels (and units if applicable)—in a small table or woven into sentences. Summaries should match variable types (e.g. proportions for categorical variables; mean/median and spread for numerical variables).


5
Short paragraph describing four interesting findings from your four variables (and/or their relationships).



Format: One side of a standard page (8.5×11 in.), readable font, complete sentences.



A code notebook (.ipynb) that contains all the tools you tried to describe the 4 variables in your dataset: there should be appropriate summaries and plots for each variable individually (univariate) and each pair of variables (bivariate), for a total of 4 univariate plots and 6 bivariate plots. Only two of these plots need to be in your one page summary.
